sentence: Subjective:

The patient, Jeremy, a 38-year-old male, presented to the office with a complaint of lower back pain that started approximately four months ago after lifting heavy boxes during his son's college move-out.
He reports that the pain has persisted despite attempting physical therapy at his gym and taking over-the-counter pain medications such as Tylenol and ibuprofen.
He denies any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms.
He also reports a history of a total knee replacement in the spring of 2020.
He is concerned about the persistent pain and numbness in his lower back and legs.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of a total knee replacement in the spring of 2020, which he reports was successful and has had no issues since.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any symptoms of bladder or bowel incontinence, fever, or chills.
He also denies any weakness or numbness in his legs except for a brief episode of numbness in his toes after the initial injury.
Current medications:
I prescribed ibuprofen for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are within normal limits, with a blood pressure of 117/56, heart rate of 68, and oxygen saturation of 95% on room air.
Physical exam:
The patient has bony protuberance of the C5-C6 vertebrae without pain to palpation.
The lumbar spine exam reveals decreased flexion and extension of the lower back, a positive straight leg raise, and spasm of the paraspinal muscles of the lumbar spine.
The musculoskeletal exam shows equal strength in the lower extremities.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: I suspect the patient has an acute disk herniation in the lumbar spine.
Problem list: Lumbar spine disk herniation; active.
Plan:
I ordered a lumbar spine MRI to further evaluate the disk herniation.
I referred the patient to a physical therapist through our health system for continued physical therapy.
I prescribed meloxicam 15mg once a day to help with pain management.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient again after the results of the lumbar spine MRI are available to discuss further treatment options, including potential steroid injections.
